questions.question: Let T: R² → R³ be a linear transformation defined by T([x, y]ᵀ) = [x+y, x-y, 2x]ᵀ. Find the standard matrix representation of T. (3 marks)
expectedPoints: Apply T to the first standard basis vector e₁ = [1,0]ᵀ: T(e₁) = [1+0, 1-0, 2*1]ᵀ = [1, 1, 2]ᵀ.
Apply T to the second standard basis vector e₂ = [0,1]ᵀ: T(e₂) = [0+1, 0-1, 2*0]ᵀ = [1, -1, 0]ᵀ.
Form the matrix by placing these results as columns: [T] = [[1, 1], [1, -1], [2, 0]].

questions.question: Let T: P₂ → P₁ be the linear transformation defined by T(a + bx + cx²) = (a + c) + (b - c)x. Consider the bases B = {1, 1+x, 1+x²} for P₂ and C = {1, x} for P₁. Find the matrix representation [T]_C^B. (8 marks)
expectedPoints: Apply T to each vector in basis B: T(1)=1+0x, T(1+x)=1+1x, T(1+x²)=2 + (-1)x.
Find the coordinate vector of each result relative to basis C. [T(1)]_C = [1, 0]ᵀ, [T(1+x)]_C = [1, 1]ᵀ, [T(1+x²)]_C = [2, -1]ᵀ.
Explain the process of finding a coordinate vector: writing the vector as a linear combination of the basis C.
Form the matrix [T]_C^B by using these coordinate vectors as columns: [[1, 1, 2], [0, 1, -1]].
Note: The basis B is not standard, so careful computation is required for each image.
Optional: Use the matrix to find T(3 - x + 2x²) by first finding its coordinates in B.
Comment on the uniqueness of the matrix representation for a given transformation and bases.
